wdigest_enabled.title: WDigest cleartext credential caching enabled
wdigest_enabled.technical_detail: When UseLogonCredential=1, the Digest Authentication security package keeps plaintext passwords in LSASS memory for the duration of the logon session. Windows 8.1+ / Server 2012 R2+ disable this by default; an explicit 1 means it was turned back on.
wdigest_enabled.risk: Any process that can read LSASS memory (SYSTEM context, or a user with SeDebugPrivilege) can extract plaintext passwords. This materially lowers the bar for credential theft in an intrusion.
wdigest_enabled.recommendation: WDigest cleartext caching should be disabled on all systems unless a specific legacy application requires it. Confirm no dependent services before changing.

runasppl_disabled.title: LSA Protection (RunAsPPL) disabled
matches: LSA Protection (RunAsPPL) registry key not found
runasppl_disabled.technical_detail: LSA Protection (Protected Process Light) runs the LSASS process under a code-integrity boundary. Non-protected processes -- even those running as SYSTEM -- cannot read LSASS memory when RunAsPPL is enabled.
runasppl_disabled.risk: Without LSA Protection, any code running as SYSTEM or with SeDebugPrivilege can read LSASS memory and extract credentials (cleartext passwords if WDigest is on, hashes, Kerberos tickets).
runasppl_disabled.recommendation: Enable LSA Protection by setting RunAsPPL to 1 (or 2 with UEFI lock on Windows 11 22H2+). Before rollout, audit the System event log for LSA-related driver incompatibilities, because incompatible drivers are prevented from loading into LSASS.

credential_guard_off.title: Credential Guard / HVCI not running
matches: Credential Guard or HVCI service is not running
Device Guard WMI class not available
credential_guard_off.technical_detail: Credential Guard uses Virtualization-Based Security (VBS) to isolate LSASS secrets in a VTL1 context that is inaccessible to the normal kernel. When running, standard LSASS memory-dumping attacks cannot reach NTLM hashes or Kerberos ticket-granting tickets.
credential_guard_off.risk: Without Credential Guard, credentials in LSASS are recoverable by any tool with SYSTEM access that can read LSASS memory. Combined with WDigest or absent LSA Protection, credential theft becomes trivial.
credential_guard_off.recommendation: Enable Credential Guard. Requires UEFI, Secure Boot, TPM 2.0, and CPU virtualization support. Validate via msinfo32 after enabling: Virtualization-based security and Credential Guard should both show Running.

mitre_attack.name: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Kerberoasting

uac_disabled.title: UAC disabled (EnableLUA=0)
matches: UAC does not filter built-in Administrator token
UAC is not filtering Administrator tokens
uac_disabled.technical_detail: EnableLUA=0 disables User Account Control entirely. All processes run with the user's full token, and there is no split-token behavior for administrators -- an administrator-equivalent account is effectively root-equivalent for every launched process.
uac_disabled.risk: Any code executed by an administrator account runs with full admin rights, bypassing the primary defense against silent elevation. Malware that executes as an admin user inherits full system privileges with no consent prompt.
uac_disabled.recommendation: Enable UAC by setting EnableLUA=1. A reboot is required. Also verify ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin is 1 or 2 and PromptOnSecureDesktop is 1. Rare systems (some kiosks, some legacy ICS) may have been configured this way deliberately -- confirm whether the policy is intentional before changing.

mitre_attack.name: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Bypass User Account Control

uac_prompt_weak.title: UAC prompt behavior relaxed
matches: UAC admin prompt may not be secure
UAC does not prompt on secure desktop
uac_prompt_weak.technical_detail: ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin values of 0, 3, 4, or 5 weaken the UAC consent prompt (auto-elevate without prompting, or prompt for credentials but without the secure desktop isolation). PromptOnSecureDesktop=0 allows malicious processes to interact with the consent prompt.
uac_prompt_weak.risk: Malware or a compromised user process can spoof or intercept the consent prompt, causing the user to unknowingly approve elevation for attacker-controlled code.
uac_prompt_weak.recommendation: Set ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin to 1 (prompt for consent on secure desktop) or 2 (prompt for consent), and PromptOnSecureDesktop to 1.

mitre_attack.name: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Bypass User Account Control

alwaysinstallelevated_both.title: AlwaysInstallElevated enabled for both HKLM and HKCU
matches: Users can install software as NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM
alwaysinstallelevated_both.technical_detail: When both HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer!AlwaysInstallElevated and HKCU\...\AlwaysInstallElevated are set to 1, Windows Installer runs any MSI with SYSTEM privileges regardless of who invokes it. Any authenticated user can craft a malicious MSI and escalate to SYSTEM.
alwaysinstallelevated_both.risk: Trivial local privilege escalation. Any unprivileged user on the system gains SYSTEM execution via a one-line MSI.
alwaysinstallelevated_both.recommendation: Disable AlwaysInstallElevated on both HKLM and HKCU, or remove the registry values entirely. Policy Object path: Computer Configuration AND User Configuration -> Administrative Templates -> Windows Components -> Windows Installer -> Always install with elevated privileges.

mitre_attack.name: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Bypass User Account Control

smbv1_enabled.title: SMBv1 server protocol enabled
smbv1_enabled.technical_detail: SMBv1 is the original Server Message Block protocol, deprecated since 2014. It has known unauthenticated-remote-code-execution vulnerabilities (MS17-010 / EternalBlue, WannaCry) and lacks modern security features (no cryptographic integrity, no pre-authentication integrity).
smbv1_enabled.risk: A network-reachable SMBv1 server is directly exposed to known-exploitable vulnerabilities. Wormable malware (WannaCry, NotPetya class) specifically targets SMBv1.
smbv1_enabled.recommendation: Disable the SMBv1 server protocol. Before rollout, check for legacy clients that still require SMBv1 -- older printers, scanners, and some ICS/OT systems (HMIs, legacy Windows-based controllers) may not support SMBv2+. Enable SMBv1 auditing (Check 27) first to discover any active SMBv1 clients.

smb_server_signing_off.title: SMB server signing not required
matches: SMB Server Require Security Signature is Disabled
smb_server_signing_off.technical_detail: When SMB server signing is not required, SMB sessions can be relayed or tampered with by an attacker positioned on the network. An attacker with LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning or a similar man-in-the-middle capability can relay the victim's authentication to arbitrary SMB targets.
smb_server_signing_off.risk: Enables SMB relay attacks (ntlmrelayx class). Combined with administrator user accounts that authenticate across the network, this leads directly to lateral movement or remote SYSTEM execution.
smb_server_signing_off.recommendation: Require SMB server signing. Verify any legacy clients (older Windows, appliances) that might break before enabling cluster-wide.

mitre_attack.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay
Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash

localaccounttokenfilterpolicy_set.title: LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy enabled (UAC remote filtering disabled)
localaccounttokenfilterpolicy_set.technical_detail: When LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy=1, UAC's default behavior of filtering local admin tokens for remote access is disabled. Local administrator accounts authenticated over the network (e.g., via SMB) receive a full admin access token instead of the filtered user token.
localaccounttokenfilterpolicy_set.risk: Enables pass-the-hash and local-admin-credential-reuse attacks. An attacker who recovers a local administrator password hash can authenticate over SMB to any other system sharing that password and execute as full admin without triggering UAC filtering.
localaccounttokenfilterpolicy_set.recommendation: Remove or set to 0 in both policies\system and Wow6432Node\...\policies\system. The default behavior (UAC token filtering for remote admin logon) should be preserved unless a specific application requires it.

mitre_attack.name: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash

lmhash_not_configured.title: LAN Manager hash storage not disabled (NoLmHash missing)
matches: NoLmHash registry key is not configured
lmhash_not_configured.technical_detail: Without NoLmHash=1, Windows stores passwords as LM hashes in addition to NTLM. LM hashes are trivially crackable: they split passwords at 7 characters, uppercase them, and use weak DES. A 14-character password cracks as two independent 7-character hashes.
lmhash_not_configured.risk: Any attacker with SAM database access (local or backed-up) can recover passwords from LM hashes in seconds to hours. Offline cracking requires no network access.
lmhash_not_configured.recommendation: Set NoLmHash=1 to prevent LM hash storage. Force a password change on all accounts afterward so existing LM hashes are cleared from the SAM.

mitre_attack.name: OS Credential Dumping: Security Account Manager

lmcompatibility_weak.title: LAN Manager authentication level allows LM or NTLMv1
matches: LM Compatability Level is not configured to prevent LM and NTLM
LM Compatability Level registry key is not configured
lmcompatibility_weak.technical_detail: LmCompatibilityLevel 5 forces the system to send NTLMv2 only and refuse LM and NTLMv1. Values below 5 allow the weaker protocols, which are crackable offline and vulnerable to relay attacks.
lmcompatibility_weak.risk: Enables offline cracking of captured network authentication. NTLMv1 has known cryptographic weaknesses that allow downgrade attacks and hash recovery.
lmcompatibility_weak.recommendation: Set LmCompatibilityLevel=5 (Send NTLMv2 response only; refuse LM and NTLM). Validate any legacy systems that still rely on LM/NTLMv1 (rare but possible on very old appliances).

mitre_attack.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay

ntlm_session_weak.title: NTLM session security does not require NTLMv2 + 128-bit
matches: NTLM Session Server Security settings is not configured to require NTLMv2
NTLM Session Client Security settings is not configured to require NTLMv2
ntlm_session_weak.technical_detail: NtlmMinServerSec and NtlmMinClientSec=0x20080030 enforce NTLMv2 session security with 128-bit encryption. Lower values allow older session-security options, including unencrypted or weakly-encrypted sessions.
ntlm_session_weak.risk: Weakly-encrypted NTLM sessions are more susceptible to offline cracking of captured authentication. Also prevents NTLM relay attacks from being downgraded to weaker crypto.
ntlm_session_weak.recommendation: Set both NtlmMinServerSec and NtlmMinClientSec to 0x20080030 (537395248) to require NTLMv2 + 128-bit encryption for session security.

mitre_attack.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay

unquoted_service_path.title: Unquoted service path with embedded space(s)
unquoted_service_path.technical_detail: A Windows service whose ImagePath contains a space and is not enclosed in quotes is parsed greedily by the Service Control Manager. If an attacker can write to an earlier space-separated path (e.g., C:\Program.exe when the service is C:\Program Files\App\service.exe), the SCM launches the attacker's binary instead.
unquoted_service_path.risk: Local privilege escalation to the service's account (often SYSTEM). Exploitation requires write access to an intermediate path, which combined with weak directory permissions elsewhere provides a reliable escalation vector.
unquoted_service_path.recommendation: Quote the ImagePath in the registry for each affected service. Vendor-installed services are the most common offenders; notify the vendor or wrap the path manually in HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\<name>!ImagePath.

mitre_attack.name: Hijack Execution Flow: Path Interception by Unquoted Path

references: Microsoft: Service Control Manager path parsing

weak_program_permissions.title: Weak ACL on program directory
weak_program_permissions.technical_detail: NTFS ACL on a directory under Program Files, Program Files (x86), or a root-level vendor directory grants Modify, Write, FullControl, WriteData, CreateFiles, AppendData, or CreateDirectories to a non-admin principal (Users, Authenticated Users, Everyone).
weak_program_permissions.risk: Any non-admin user can replace program binaries, DLLs, or configuration files. When those files are later executed by a privileged process (service, scheduled task, administrator manually running the program), the attacker gains execution in that privileged context.
weak_program_permissions.recommendation: Tighten the ACL so only Administrators and SYSTEM have Modify/Write. Users typically need only Read & Execute. If the vendor application requires write access to its install folder for logs or config, move those files to %ProgramData%\<Vendor> and restrict the install folder.

mitre_attack.name: Hijack Execution Flow: Executable Installer File Permissions Weakness
Hijack Execution Flow: Services File Permissions Weakness

compilers_present.title: Compilers or build tools present on system
compilers_present.technical_detail: One or more compilers, assemblers, or build tools were detected (GCC, MinGW, clang, MSVC cl.exe, NASM, make, cmake, Strawberry Perl, Python). These enable local compilation on the system.
compilers_present.risk: An attacker with code execution on the box can compile arbitrary binaries locally, bypassing antivirus signature detection that would catch a pre-built payload downloaded from the network. This is a living-off-the-land enabler.
compilers_present.recommendation: Remove compilers from systems that do not need them (operator workstations, HMIs, general-purpose servers). On engineering workstations or systems that genuinely require build capability, document the business justification, restrict binary ACLs to specific users, and consider AppLocker/WDAC rules to restrict execution by the broader user population.

mitre_attack.name: Obfuscated Files or Information: Compile After Delivery

ps_module_logging_off.title: PowerShell Module Logging not enabled
matches: PowerShell Module Logging is not enabled
ps_module_logging_off.technical_detail: PowerShell Module Logging records cmdlet invocations and parameter values from specified modules to Event ID 4103. Without it, a forensic analyst cannot see which cmdlets executed or with what arguments.
ps_module_logging_off.risk: PowerShell-borne attacks are much harder to detect and investigate. Threat actors heavily use PowerShell for post-compromise operations; without logging, the forensic trail is largely gone.
ps_module_logging_off.recommendation: Enable Module Logging via GPO (Computer Configuration -> Administrative Templates -> Windows Components -> Windows PowerShell -> Turn on Module Logging). Configure module names as * (all modules).

mitre_attack.name: Impair Defenses: Disable Windows Event Logging

ps_scriptblock_logging_off.title: PowerShell Script Block Logging not enabled
matches: Script Block Logging is not enabled
ps_scriptblock_logging_off.technical_detail: Script Block Logging records the full content of every PowerShell script block that executes, to Event ID 4104. This captures obfuscated commands, in-memory code, and dynamically-generated scripts that other logging methods miss.
ps_scriptblock_logging_off.risk: The single most useful PowerShell logging mechanism for threat hunting and forensics. Without it, obfuscated PowerShell attacks are largely invisible in the event log.
ps_scriptblock_logging_off.recommendation: Enable Script Block Logging via GPO (Computer Configuration -> Administrative Templates -> Windows Components -> Windows PowerShell -> Turn on PowerShell Script Block Logging). Also enable invocation logging (separate sub-setting).

mitre_attack.name: Impair Defenses: Disable Windows Event Logging

cmdline_auditing_off.title: Command-line auditing not enabled
matches: Command-line process auditing is not enabled
cmdline_auditing_off.technical_detail: ProcessCreationIncludeCmdLine_Enabled=1 causes Event ID 4688 (process creation) to include the full command line in the event record. Without it, only the executable name and parent process are logged, not the arguments.
cmdline_auditing_off.risk: Command-line arguments are often the most informative part of a process-creation event. Malicious invocations like `powershell -enc <base64>` or `cmd /c net user admin /add /domain` are invisible without this setting.
cmdline_auditing_off.recommendation: Enable via GPO (Computer Configuration -> Administrative Templates -> System -> Audit Process Creation -> Include command line in process creation events). Also confirm Process Creation auditing is enabled under Advanced Audit Policy Configuration.

mitre_attack.name: Impair Defenses: Disable Windows Event Logging

missing_critical_patches.title: Missing critical or important Windows updates
matches: Missing Critical or Important Update KB:
missing_critical_patches.technical_detail: Windows Update has identified one or more updates classified as Critical or Important that are not installed on this system. These typically patch publicly-documented remote-code-execution or privilege-escalation vulnerabilities.
missing_critical_patches.risk: Unpatched vulnerabilities with known exploits provide direct initial-access paths. Attackers scan for unpatched systems; publicly-released proof-of-concept code lets commodity threat actors weaponize an un-applied patch within days of release.
missing_critical_patches.recommendation: Apply all missing Critical and Important updates. For OT systems under a maintenance window, schedule the cumulative update for the next approved window; for workstations, deploy immediately. Validate applied patches via `Get-HotFix` or Windows Update history after reboot.

autoupdate_not_scheduled.title: Windows Automatic Updates not set to scheduled install
matches: Windows AutoUpdate is not configured to automatically install updates
autoupdate_not_scheduled.technical_detail: AUOptions controls Windows Update behavior. Value 4 (Auto download and schedule install) is the hardened default. Values 0-3 leave patching to manual intervention or user prompts, which frequently delays security updates by weeks.
autoupdate_not_scheduled.risk: Systems without automatic patching remain vulnerable to publicly-documented exploits well after Microsoft ships fixes. Attackers scan for unpatched Windows versions and use N-day exploits against them.
autoupdate_not_scheduled.recommendation: Set AUOptions=4 (Auto download and schedule installation) and configure a scheduled install time that aligns with the system's maintenance window. For OT systems, WSUS or a managed patch process is preferable to auto-install -- but unmanaged systems should still be on auto-install rather than manual.

bitlocker_not_encrypted.title: BitLocker not enabled on operating system volume
matches: BitLocker not detected on Operating System Volume
BitLocker service (BDESVC) is installed but stopped
BitLocker check skipped: neither Get-BitLockerVolume nor manage-bde.exe available
bitlocker_not_encrypted.technical_detail: BitLocker full-disk encryption protects the operating system volume at rest. Without it, physical access to the drive (or an image of it) yields direct access to all data and to the offline SAM database for credential recovery.
bitlocker_not_encrypted.risk: A stolen laptop, decommissioned drive, or improperly wiped system leaks all data stored on the volume. Offline attacks against the SAM (dumping LM/NTLM hashes, tampering with boot files for bypass) become trivial without disk encryption.
bitlocker_not_encrypted.recommendation: Enable BitLocker on the OS volume using a TPM-backed key protector. Back up recovery keys to Active Directory or a secure escrow system before enabling. On Server editions, the BitLocker feature must first be installed.

mitre_attack.name: OS Credential Dumping: Security Account Manager

exploit_protection_off.title: Exploit Protection mitigations (DEP/ASLR/CFG) not enabled
matches: System-wide DEP (Data Execution Prevention) is not enabled
System-wide mandatory ASLR is not enabled
System-wide Control Flow Guard (CFG) is not enabled
exploit_protection_off.technical_detail: Data Execution Prevention, mandatory ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization), and Control Flow Guard are foundational exploit mitigations built into modern Windows. Each makes memory-corruption exploits (buffer overflow, use-after-free, ROP chain construction) substantially harder.
exploit_protection_off.risk: Without these mitigations, memory-corruption vulnerabilities in any process running on the system become much more exploitable. Older malware families and commodity exploits specifically target systems with mitigations disabled.
exploit_protection_off.recommendation: Enable system-wide Exploit Protection: `Set-ProcessMitigation -System -Enable DEP,SEHOP,BottomUp,ForceRelocateImages,CFG`. The default Windows 10/11 configuration has these on -- a negative finding means a policy has explicitly disabled them.

laps_not_installed.title: LAPS (Local Administrator Password Solution) not installed
matches: Local Administrator Password Solution (LAPS) is not installed
LAPS does not appear to be configured
laps_not_installed.technical_detail: LAPS randomizes and rotates the local administrator password on each managed system and stores it in a securely-ACLed directory attribute (AD or Azure AD). Without LAPS, local administrator passwords tend to be identical across systems or rarely rotated.
laps_not_installed.risk: Shared local administrator passwords enable lateral movement: a single compromised host discloses the password to every other system using the same local admin. This is one of the most common paths from initial access to domain-wide compromise.
laps_not_installed.recommendation: Deploy Windows LAPS (built into Windows 10 21H2+ and Server 2019+) or legacy LAPS on all managed systems. Configure password complexity and rotation interval per organizational policy.

mitre_attack.name: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash

applocker_not_configured.title: AppLocker not configured
applocker_not_configured.technical_detail: AppLocker (or WDAC) is Windows' application allowlisting mechanism. When configured in enforce mode, only signed or explicitly-allowed binaries, scripts, and DLLs execute. Without an allowlist policy, any executable the user (or malware) can place on disk will run.
applocker_not_configured.risk: Unchecked execution of arbitrary binaries and scripts. Malware delivered via email, download, or lateral movement runs without any code-integrity check. This is the single most impactful missing control on most Windows endpoints.
applocker_not_configured.recommendation: Deploy AppLocker or WDAC in enforce mode. AppLocker is simpler to roll out; WDAC is stronger (kernel-enforced) and preferred for high-security environments. Start in audit mode to build a baseline of legitimate executables before switching to enforce.

netsessionenum_open.title: Net Session Enumeration not restricted
matches: RestrictRemoteSAM registry key is not configured
SrvsvcSessionInfo registry key is not configured
netsessionenum_open.technical_detail: SrvsvcSessionInfo and RestrictRemoteSAM registry keys control which principals can enumerate remote sessions and the SAM database. By default (unconfigured), any authenticated user can enumerate active logon sessions via NetSessionEnum and SAMR.
netsessionenum_open.risk: Attackers and AD enumeration tools (BloodHound, PowerView) use these interfaces to map user-to-host sessions and identify high-value targets (domain admins logged into specific workstations). Tightening these controls meaningfully slows AD enumeration.
netsessionenum_open.recommendation: Set RestrictRemoteSAM to a restrictive SDDL that only permits Administrators. Apply the Net Cease hardening for SrvsvcSessionInfo. Both changes have minimal impact on legitimate use.

device_guard_not_configured.title: Device Guard not configured
matches: Device Guard: no properties exist and therefore is not configured
Device Guard WMI class not available
device_guard_not_configured.technical_detail: Device Guard uses Virtualization-Based Security (VBS) to enforce code-integrity policies at the kernel level. Hypervisor-Protected Code Integrity (HVCI) prevents unsigned or policy-violating code from executing in kernel mode.
device_guard_not_configured.risk: Without Device Guard, a kernel-mode exploit or signed-driver attack (bring-your-own-vulnerable-driver) can run arbitrary kernel code, disable security products, and persist below the operating system.
device_guard_not_configured.recommendation: Enable Device Guard with HVCI. Requires UEFI, Secure Boot, TPM 2.0, and VBS-capable CPU. Test driver compatibility before rollout -- incompatible drivers will be blocked from loading.

account_policy_weak.title: Account lockout or password policy not hardened
matches: Account lockout threshold is not configured
Minimum password length is too short
Built-in Administrator account has not been renamed
account_policy_weak.technical_detail: Weak or missing account policies (no lockout threshold, short password minimum, Guest account enabled, default 'Administrator' name) create multiple low-friction paths for account compromise: password spray, default-credential guessing, and brute force.
account_policy_weak.risk: Password-spray attacks against the built-in Administrator account are a primary initial-access technique. No lockout threshold turns this into unlimited attempts. Short password minimums accelerate offline cracking of any hash capture.
account_policy_weak.recommendation: Configure: lockout threshold 5 with 15-minute duration; minimum password length 14; disable the Guest account; rename the built-in Administrator account from its default name. Policies should be set via GPO for consistency across the fleet.

secure_boot_off.title: Secure Boot not enabled
matches: Secure Boot status could not be determined
secure_boot_off.technical_detail: UEFI Secure Boot verifies firmware and OS loader signatures before allowing boot. Without it, an attacker with persistence (bootkit, modified bootloader) can load unsigned or altered boot code that executes before the OS.
secure_boot_off.risk: Enables bootkit persistence and pre-OS tampering. Once a bootkit is in place, it can disable Credential Guard, HVCI, kernel driver signing, and similar boot-time protections before they initialize.
secure_boot_off.recommendation: Enable Secure Boot in UEFI firmware. Requires the disk to be GPT-partitioned and Windows installed in UEFI mode. On BIOS-mode systems, conversion with `mbr2gpt.exe` is required and should be tested.

risky_service_running.title: High-risk service running (Print Spooler, Remote Registry, SNMP, etc.)
matches: Print Spooler (PrintNightmare risk) service is running
service is RUNNING. Evaluate whether this service is needed
risky_service_running.technical_detail: Services like Print Spooler (PrintNightmare CVE-2021-34527), Remote Registry, SNMP (community string enumeration), Telnet Server (unencrypted credentials), and Routing and Remote Access expose attack surface. Each has known exploitation paths and is rarely needed outside specific roles.
risky_service_running.risk: Print Spooler has produced multiple wormable RCE vulnerabilities in the past 5 years. SNMP enumeration discloses system inventory to the network. Telnet leaks credentials in cleartext. Each enabled service is an uncontested attack surface.
risky_service_running.recommendation: Disable services that the system's role does not require. A standard user workstation typically does not need Remote Registry, SNMP, Telnet, or Remote Access. Print Spooler should be disabled on systems that do not print (most servers, many specialized workstations).

smb_encryption_off.title: SMB encryption / unencrypted access not hardened
smb_encryption_off.technical_detail: SMBv3 supports end-to-end encryption of the protocol. EncryptData and RejectUnencryptedAccess, when enabled, force clients to use encrypted SMB and reject legacy unencrypted sessions.
smb_encryption_off.risk: Unencrypted SMB is subject to passive interception on the local network. Combined with weak or absent signing, it is also subject to active manipulation (ntlmrelayx).
smb_encryption_off.recommendation: Enable SMB encryption on servers hosting sensitive file shares. Consider RejectUnencryptedAccess for high-security environments. Verify legacy client compatibility before enabling cluster-wide -- some older clients and appliances do not support SMBv3 encryption.

mitre_attack.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay

smbv1_audit_off.title: SMBv1 auditing disabled
smbv1_audit_off.technical_detail: With SMBv1 auditing enabled, any SMBv1 session attempt is logged. This is how an administrator discovers legacy clients still depending on SMBv1 before disabling the protocol entirely.
smbv1_audit_off.risk: Without auditing, disabling SMBv1 cleanly requires guesswork about which clients still need it. In practice, organizations either leave SMBv1 on (risk) or disable it without audit (break legacy applications unexpectedly).
smbv1_audit_off.recommendation: Enable SMBv1 auditing on servers. Monitor the event log for 30-90 days to identify legacy clients. Then disable the protocol once all legitimate users have been migrated.

anonymous_enumeration.title: Anonymous enumeration of SAM or shares not restricted
matches: RestrictAnonymous registry key is not configured
RestrictAnonymoussam registry key is not configured
anonymous_enumeration.technical_detail: RestrictAnonymous and RestrictAnonymousSAM control whether unauthenticated users can enumerate local accounts, groups, and shares via SAMR and LSARPC. With default (unconfigured) values, an anonymous network session can dump the full local user and group database.
anonymous_enumeration.risk: Anonymous enumeration reveals user account names, group membership, and share names without authentication. This is reconnaissance data used for password spray target selection and lateral movement planning.
anonymous_enumeration.recommendation: Set RestrictAnonymous=1 and RestrictAnonymousSAM=1 via GPO. Default for modern domain-joined systems is typically hardened; standalone systems may still have the legacy (open) behavior.

untrusted_fonts_off.title: Kernel untrusted-font blocking not configured
matches: Kernel MitigationOptions key does not exist
Kernel MitigationOptions key is configured not to block
untrusted_fonts_off.technical_detail: On Windows 10+, a registry-configured MitigationOptions bitmask blocks kernel loading of untrusted fonts. Font parsers historically have been a kernel-mode attack surface (multiple CVEs).
untrusted_fonts_off.risk: Without untrusted-font blocking, a malicious font file processed by kernel-mode components can reach exploitable parser code. Combined with user-mode delivery (web page, document) this becomes a kernel exploit vector.
untrusted_fonts_off.recommendation: Configure MitigationOptions to block untrusted fonts kernel-wide. Less critical on modern Windows since font parsing moved to user mode in many components, but still a defensive layer.

asr_not_configured.title: Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rules not configured
matches: No Attack Surface Reduction rules configured
asr_not_configured.technical_detail: ASR rules are Windows Defender Exploit Guard policies that block common attack techniques: Office macro child processes, credential theft from LSASS, obfuscated scripts, executable email attachments, and similar. Each rule is independently configurable.
asr_not_configured.risk: ASR rules are among the highest-leverage Windows endpoint hardening controls available. A typical initial-access chain (phishing email -> Office macro -> LOLBin -> credential theft) is broken at multiple points by ASR rules. Leaving them unconfigured passes up a significant defense.
asr_not_configured.recommendation: Enable key ASR rules in Block mode: `Block credential stealing from LSASS`, `Block Office applications from creating child processes`, `Block executable content from email client`, `Block Win32 API calls from Office macros`. Start in Audit mode, review exclusions, then move to Block.

smb_client_signing_off.title: SMB client signing not required
matches: SMB Client Require Security Signature is Disabled
smb_client_signing_off.technical_detail: SMB client signing prevents an on-path attacker from tampering with SMB sessions the client initiates. Without it, the client will accept unsigned responses, enabling relay attacks back to the client.
smb_client_signing_off.risk: Enables SMB relay attacks against the client: an attacker that captures or coerces authentication can relay it to other targets the client connects to. Combined with unsigned server connections, this is a reliable lateral-movement path.
smb_client_signing_off.recommendation: Set RequireSecuritySignature=1 on the LanmanWorkstation parameters. GPO: `Microsoft network client: Digitally sign communications (always)` -> Enabled.

mitre_attack.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay

audit_policy_missing.title: Audit policy missing key subcategories
matches: Audit policy for Process creation events: No Auditing
Audit policy for Security system changes: No Auditing
Audit policy for Account Logon events: No Auditing
Audit policy for Security group changes: No Auditing
Audit policy for User account changes: No Auditing
audit_policy_missing.technical_detail: Advanced Audit Policy Configuration has dozens of subcategories. Only certain subcategories produce the events needed for meaningful detection and forensics: Process Creation, Logon/Logoff, Special Logon, User Account Management, Security Group Management, Security System Extension, Authentication Policy Change, and a handful of others.
audit_policy_missing.risk: Missing audit subcategories create blind spots. If Process Creation auditing is off, Event ID 4688 is not produced at all, making post-compromise reconstruction impossible regardless of command-line auditing (Check 62) state.
audit_policy_missing.recommendation: Configure via GPO under Computer Configuration -> Windows Settings -> Security Settings -> Advanced Audit Policy Configuration. Reference Microsoft's recommended baseline or CIS Benchmark 17.x for specific subcategory settings.

mitre_attack.name: Impair Defenses: Disable Windows Event Logging

localadmin_group_crowded.title: Local Administrators group has more than one member
matches: More than one account is in local Administrators group
accounts in local Administrators group. Review membership
localadmin_group_crowded.technical_detail: The local Administrators group grants full administrative control of the system. Each additional member is another account-compromise path to full admin.
localadmin_group_crowded.risk: Extra accounts in the local Administrators group typically represent stale admin users, service accounts that no longer need admin, or shared-team accounts. Each is a lateral-movement opportunity.
localadmin_group_crowded.recommendation: Review membership. Remove stale accounts, service accounts that no longer need elevated rights, and shared-team accounts. Where domain admin access is required, use a dedicated admin account or Just-In-Time elevation.

cached_logons_high.title: Cached logons count too high
matches: CachedLogonsCount Is Not Set to 0 or 1
cached_logons_high.technical_detail: CachedLogonsCount controls how many domain logons are cached locally so users can authenticate when the domain controller is unreachable. Each cached logon stores a derived credential (mscache / mscash2) on disk.
cached_logons_high.risk: Cached credentials are extractable with SYSTEM access and crackable offline. While mscache2 is slower to crack than NTLM hashes, the attacker only needs one cached credential to begin lateral movement.
cached_logons_high.recommendation: Set CachedLogonsCount to 0 (no cache) on systems that do not require offline authentication, or 1-4 on systems that occasionally operate disconnected. Default value of 10 is excessive for most roles.

wdigest_unset.title: WDigest UseLogonCredential not explicitly disabled
matches: WDigest UseLogonCredential key does not exist
wdigest_unset.technical_detail: Windows 8.1+ / Server 2012 R2+ disable WDigest cleartext caching by default when the UseLogonCredential key is absent. However, leaving the key unset means a future policy change (intentional or otherwise) could re-enable cleartext caching without a corrective control in place.
wdigest_unset.risk: Relies on OS default behavior for a critical credential-protection setting. Lower risk than an explicit Enabled value (see wdigest_enabled), but still worth hardening for defense in depth.
wdigest_unset.recommendation: Explicitly set UseLogonCredential=0 to make the hardened state policy-driven rather than default-dependent.

restrictremoteclients_unset.title: RestrictRemoteClients RPC policy not configured
matches: RestrictRemoteClients registry key is not configured
restrictremoteclients_unset.technical_detail: RestrictRemoteClients=1 requires authentication for all RPC calls to the endpoint mapper. Without it, unauthenticated clients can query the RPC endpoint mapper for available services.
restrictremoteclients_unset.risk: Unauthenticated RPC enumeration is a reconnaissance vector. Combined with weaknesses in any exposed RPC interface, it can also enable relay or unauthenticated exploit attempts.
restrictremoteclients_unset.recommendation: Set RestrictRemoteClients=1 via GPO: Computer Configuration -> Administrative Templates -> System -> Remote Procedure Call -> Restrict Unauthenticated RPC clients -> Enabled, Authenticated.

rdp_nla_not_required.title: RDP Network Level Authentication not required
matches: RDP Network Level Authentication (NLA) is not required
UserAuthentication is not set to require NLA
rdp_nla_not_required.technical_detail: Network Level Authentication (NLA) requires the client to authenticate before establishing an RDP session. Without NLA, the RDP server exposes more of the protocol to unauthenticated clients, including pre-auth code paths that have historically been exploitable (BlueKeep CVE-2019-0708).
rdp_nla_not_required.risk: Enables unauthenticated pre-auth exploitation of RDP vulnerabilities, including BlueKeep-class worms. Also increases exposure to resource-exhaustion denial-of-service against the RDP service.
rdp_nla_not_required.recommendation: Set UserAuthentication=1 in HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server\WinStations\RDP-Tcp. GPO: `Require user authentication for remote connections by using Network Level Authentication` -> Enabled.

rdp_allowed_when_not_needed.title: RDP accepting remote connections
matches: AllowRemoteRPC should be disabled to deny RDP
fDenyTSConnections should be set to not allow remote connections
rdp_allowed_when_not_needed.technical_detail: AllowRemoteRPC=1 enables the RDP service endpoint. fDenyTSConnections=0 allows remote RDP connections to complete. Together they make the system reachable as an RDP target.
rdp_allowed_when_not_needed.risk: If RDP is not required for the system's role, its presence is unnecessary attack surface. RDP-exposed systems are primary targets for credential-guessing, BlueKeep-class exploits, and ransomware initial access.
rdp_allowed_when_not_needed.recommendation: If RDP is not needed, set fDenyTSConnections=1 and AllowRemoteRPC=0. If RDP is needed, ensure NLA is required (see Check 42), minimum encryption is High or FIPS, source IPs are restricted by firewall, and the exposed accounts follow strong password / account-lockout policy.

rdp_encryption_low.title: RDP minimum encryption level below High
matches: RDP minimum encryption level is below High
rdp_encryption_low.technical_detail: MinEncryptionLevel controls the minimum encryption strength accepted for RDP sessions. Value 1 is Low (56-bit), 2 is Client Compatible, 3 is High (128-bit), 4 is FIPS-compliant.
rdp_encryption_low.risk: Weak RDP encryption permits session interception or downgrade attacks. Modern clients support 128-bit encryption; legacy clients that cannot are generally ones that shouldn't be used anyway.
rdp_encryption_low.recommendation: Set MinEncryptionLevel=3 (High) or 4 (FIPS). Test legacy RDP clients (appliances, thin clients, older Windows) before deploying cluster-wide.

llmnr_enabled.title: LLMNR enabled
matches: DNSClient.EnableMulticast does not exist or is enabled
EnableMulticast not configured. LLMNR is likely enabled
llmnr_enabled.technical_detail: Link-Local Multicast Name Resolution (LLMNR) is a fallback name-resolution protocol used when DNS fails. It is broadcast-based and unauthenticated: any host on the same broadcast domain can respond to LLMNR queries.
llmnr_enabled.risk: LLMNR spoofing (Responder, Inveigh) captures NTLM authentication when a client fails DNS resolution. Combined with SMB relay and SMB signing not required, this is a well-documented path to network-wide credential theft and lateral movement.
llmnr_enabled.recommendation: Disable LLMNR by setting EnableMulticast=0 via GPO: Computer Configuration -> Administrative Templates -> Network -> DNS Client -> Turn off multicast name resolution -> Enabled.

mitre_attack.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay

netbios_enabled.title: NetBIOS over TCP/IP enabled
netbios_enabled.technical_detail: NetBIOS Name Service (NBT-NS) is a legacy broadcast-based name resolution protocol. Like LLMNR, it is unauthenticated and any host on the local subnet can respond to queries.
netbios_enabled.risk: NBT-NS spoofing is functionally equivalent to LLMNR poisoning for credential capture and relay. Both are commonly exploited together by the same tooling. Disabling LLMNR without also disabling NBT-NS leaves one half of the attack surface open.
netbios_enabled.recommendation: Set TcpipNetbiosOptions=2 (disable NetBIOS over TCP/IP) per network adapter via registry, DHCP scope option, or adapter GUI (TCP/IPv4 -> Advanced -> WINS -> Disable NetBIOS over TCP/IP).

mitre_attack.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay

computer_browser_running.title: Computer Browser service running
computer_browser_running.technical_detail: The Computer Browser service maintains and distributes a list of resources on the local network using the legacy SMB Master Browser protocol. It is not needed on modern networks that use Active Directory or DNS for resource discovery.
computer_browser_running.risk: Legacy protocol that discloses network resource inventory. Unnecessary attack surface on systems that do not require master-browser functionality (which is virtually all modern systems).
computer_browser_running.recommendation: Stop and disable the Browser service. Set startup type to Disabled. Applies to nearly all Windows systems in a managed environment.

wpad_not_hardened.title: WPAD not hardened
matches: WpadOverride registry key is configured to allow WPAD
KB3165191 to harden WPAD is not installed
wpad_not_hardened.technical_detail: Web Proxy Auto-Discovery (WPAD) attempts to locate a proxy configuration via DHCP option or DNS. The lookup can be spoofed via LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning, causing the client to fetch a malicious proxy configuration that intercepts all HTTP(S) traffic.
wpad_not_hardened.risk: WPAD poisoning redirects all HTTP(S) traffic through an attacker-controlled proxy. Credentials (including NTLM challenges to attacker-controlled URLs), session tokens, and application traffic are all exposed.
wpad_not_hardened.recommendation: Multi-step hardening: (1) add `255.255.255.255 wpad` entry to %SystemRoot%\System32\drivers\etc\hosts (or block wpad at DNS); (2) disable the WinHttpAutoProxySvc service; (3) apply KB3165191 or a superseding cumulative update; (4) set WpadOverride registry key to 1.

mitre_attack.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay

wins_lmhosts_enabled.title: WINS / LMHOSTS legacy name resolution enabled
wins_lmhosts_enabled.technical_detail: WINS and LMHOSTS lookup are legacy NetBIOS name resolution mechanisms. Both are broadcast-based and unauthenticated in the same way as LLMNR and NBT-NS, making them additional spoofing targets.
wins_lmhosts_enabled.risk: Additional surface for name-resolution spoofing attacks. Each enabled legacy mechanism is another path for tools like Responder to coerce authentication.
wins_lmhosts_enabled.recommendation: Disable WINS resolution and LMHOSTS lookup per network adapter. In adapter properties: TCP/IPv4 -> Advanced -> WINS tab -> uncheck both options. Or set DHCP scope options accordingly.

mitre_attack.name: Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay

ipv6_enabled_unused.title: IPv6 interface assigned
ipv6_enabled_unused.technical_detail: An IPv6 address has been assigned to a network interface. If IPv6 is not in operational use on the network, this creates a monitoring blind spot: IPv6 DHCP and router advertisement attacks can reach the host without being caught by IPv4-only monitoring.
ipv6_enabled_unused.risk: Unused IPv6 on networks without IPv6 monitoring creates a parallel attack path (mitm6 tool class) for name-resolution poisoning and credential relay. Also enables inadvertent IPv6 egress if the network supports it.
ipv6_enabled_unused.recommendation: If IPv6 is not used in the environment, disable the IPv6 binding on the adapter: `Disable-NetAdapterBinding -Name <adapter> -ComponentID ms_tcpip6`. If IPv6 is used, ensure monitoring and DHCP/RA controls cover IPv6 as well as IPv4.

icmp_redirect_enabled.title: ICMP redirects accepted
icmp_redirect_enabled.technical_detail: An ICMP Redirect message tells a host to use a different gateway for specific destinations. When accepted, they let a local-network attacker redirect traffic via an attacker-controlled gateway.
icmp_redirect_enabled.risk: Local-network traffic redirection: an attacker on the same subnet can MITM specific routes by sending crafted ICMP redirects. Less common attack than LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning but still occasionally used.
icmp_redirect_enabled.recommendation: Set EnableICMPRedirect=0 in HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip\Parameters. Also consider DisableIPSourceRouting=2 and PerformRouterDiscovery=0 while tightening the TCP/IP stack.

tcpip_source_routing_on.title: IP source routing not disabled
matches: IP source routing is not fully disabled
tcpip_source_routing_on.technical_detail: IP source routing allows the sender to specify the route a packet takes through the network. This legacy feature is rarely needed and has been abused for bypassing firewalls and reflection attacks.
tcpip_source_routing_on.risk: Enables firewall bypass and reflection-style attacks against hosts that accept source-routed packets. Low practical risk on modern networks but still worth closing as a hardening layer.
tcpip_source_routing_on.recommendation: Set DisableIPSourceRouting=2 (highest protection: drop all source-routed packets) in HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Tcpip\Parameters.

psv2_enabled.title: PowerShell v2 feature enabled
matches: PowerShell Version 2 should be disabled: Enabled
psv2_enabled.technical_detail: The Windows optional feature `MicrosoftWindowsPowerShellV2` is enabled on this system. PowerShell v2 predates script block logging, module logging, and AMSI integration. A PS v5.1+ session can be downgraded via `powershell -Version 2` to run without any of these logging controls.
psv2_enabled.risk: PowerShell downgrade attack: any malicious script can invoke `powershell -Version 2 -Command ...` to execute with logging and AMSI disabled, defeating most PowerShell-based detection.
psv2_enabled.recommendation: Disable the v2 engine: `Disable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName MicrosoftWindowsPowerShellV2,MicrosoftWindowsPowerShellV2Root`. Also consider uninstalling .NET Framework 2.0/3.5 if no applications require it.

ps_language_not_constrained.title: PowerShell Language Mode is not ConstrainedLanguage
matches: Execution Langugage Mode Is Not ConstrainedLanguage
Execution Language Mode Is Not ConstrainedLanguage
ps_language_not_constrained.technical_detail: PowerShell's Language Mode controls what the script engine allows. FullLanguage permits arbitrary .NET API invocation, including direct Win32 calls used by most advanced offensive PowerShell tools. ConstrainedLanguage restricts scripts to a safe subset.
ps_language_not_constrained.risk: Full Language Mode is a prerequisite for most in-memory offensive PowerShell frameworks (PowerSploit, Empire, Nishang). Restricting to ConstrainedLanguage neutralizes a large fraction of attacker tooling.
ps_language_not_constrained.recommendation: Deploy AppLocker or WDAC in enforce mode. PowerShell automatically switches to ConstrainedLanguage when a script-control policy is active on the system. Manually setting `$ExecutionContext.SessionState.LanguageMode` is not a durable control.

ps_transcription_off.title: PowerShell Transcription not enabled
ps_transcription_off.technical_detail: PowerShell Transcription records every PowerShell session's input and output to a text file in a configured OutputDirectory. It captures what Script Block Logging does not: the full interactive session including command output.
ps_transcription_off.risk: Without transcription, an investigator has the commands that ran (via Script Block Logging, if enabled) but not the output -- making it harder to reconstruct what data was accessed, what was returned, and whether commands succeeded.
ps_transcription_off.recommendation: Enable Transcription via GPO (Turn on PowerShell Transcription -> Enabled). Set OutputDirectory to a write-only share or a tamper-resistant local path. Enable InvocationHeader for timestamped output.

mitre_attack.name: Impair Defenses: Disable Windows Event Logging

ps_protected_event_logging_off.title: PowerShell Protected Event Logging not enabled
ps_protected_event_logging_off.technical_detail: Protected Event Logging encrypts event data locally using a public key, decryptable only by a controlled private key. When enabled, script block logs can contain sensitive content (parameters, secrets, intermediate variables) that would otherwise be recoverable by anyone with local admin.
ps_protected_event_logging_off.risk: Unprotected PowerShell event logs can contain credential material, API keys, and other sensitive strings encountered during normal operation. An attacker with local admin reads these in plaintext after compromise.
ps_protected_event_logging_off.recommendation: Deploy a Protected Event Logging certificate (see Microsoft's documentation) and enable the GPO: Computer Configuration -> Administrative Templates -> Windows Components -> Windows PowerShell -> Turn On Protected Event Logging.

winrm_running_without_hardening.title: WinRM service running
matches: WinRM Services is running and may be accepting connections
winrm_running_without_hardening.technical_detail: Windows Remote Management (WinRM) enables remote PowerShell execution and management. When running with default configuration, it accepts connections on TCP 5985 (HTTP) or 5986 (HTTPS) from any host matching the TrustedHosts list.
winrm_running_without_hardening.risk: WinRM is a primary lateral-movement vector (Invoke-Command, Enter-PSSession). Combined with pass-the-hash or credential reuse, a single compromised admin credential enables remote execution across the WinRM-reachable fleet.
winrm_running_without_hardening.recommendation: If WinRM is not in active use, disable it: `Disable-PSRemoting -Force`; `Set-Service -Name WinRM -StartupType Disabled`; disable the `Windows Remote Management (HTTP-In)` firewall rule. If WinRM is needed, restrict TrustedHosts, require HTTPS with a real certificate, and restrict source IPs via firewall.

event_log_size_small.title: Event log maximum size too small
matches: max log size is smaller than
event_log_size_small.technical_detail: Event log maximum size determines how many events can be retained before older events are overwritten. On active systems, small logs roll over within hours, destroying forensic evidence before it can be collected.
event_log_size_small.risk: During incident response, relevant events may already have rolled out of the log. Attackers can also accelerate rollover by generating noise events to push forensic evidence out of the ring buffer.
event_log_size_small.recommendation: Set Application, System, Security, and Windows PowerShell logs to 4 GB. Set operational logs (PowerShell/Operational, TerminalServices, TaskScheduler, SMBServer/Audit, Security-Netlogon, WinRM, WMI-Activity) to 1 GB each. Use `wevtutil sl <log> /ms:<bytes>` or GPO under Event Log Service. Also configure log forwarding so events survive local rollover.

mitre_attack.name: Indicator Removal: Clear Windows Event Logs

wsh_enabled.title: Windows Script Host enabled
matches: WSH Setting Enabled key is Enabled
WSH Setting Enabled key does not exist
WSH Setting Enabled key does not exist.
wsh_enabled.technical_detail: Windows Script Host (WSH) is the execution environment for VBScript, JScript, and legacy Windows scripting. It is a well-worn malware execution path (.vbs / .js email attachments, macros that drop WSH scripts) and rarely needed on modern systems.
wsh_enabled.risk: WSH is a common initial-access execution vector. Malware delivered via email or macro frequently drops a .vbs or .js file and executes it via wscript.exe / cscript.exe, bypassing AppLocker rules that only cover .exe/.dll.
wsh_enabled.recommendation: Disable WSH by setting HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows Script Host\Settings!Enabled=0 (DWORD). Verify no in-house scripts depend on WSH before deploying cluster-wide.

mitre_attack.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Visual Basic

kb_security_patch_missing.title: Legacy security hotfix (KB2871997 or KB3165191) not installed
matches: KB3165191 to harden WPAD is not installed
kb_security_patch_missing.technical_detail: KB2871997 (2014) added credential-handling improvements that harden LSASS against WDigest-style caching. KB3165191 (2016) is the WPAD hardening hotfix. Both are old; on current Windows, their content is superseded by cumulative updates.
kb_security_patch_missing.risk: Missing either indicates the system is either very out-of-date or the cumulative update baseline has drifted. On current Windows, these specific KBs are superseded and the absence is typically benign -- but the finding is a signal of patch-management health.
kb_security_patch_missing.recommendation: Apply current Windows cumulative updates. The hotfix content is included in modern baselines. If a fully-patched modern system still flags these, it's a reporting artifact rather than a real gap.
